title: Personalized News Feed - System Design Interview
tagline: Design an ML-powered feed ranking system that serves personalized posts within a 200 ms latency budget.
problem: Build a personalized news feed that ranks unseen posts and posts with unseen comments for billions of users, optimizing engagement signals such as clicks, likes, comments, shares, hides, blocks, and connection requests.

functional: Generate a personalized feed for each user.
Rank posts by predicted engagement and quality.
Use user, post, social, and interaction signals.
Log impressions and reactions for training.
Serve feed responses in under 200 ms.
nonFunctional: Fresh features for users and posts.
Controls for negative feedback, diversity, and ads.
requirements.designPrompt: This is an ML system design interview. Keep candidate generation, feature logging, model training, online ranking, and feedback loops distinct.

capacity.label: Daily feed sessions
capacity.value: About 4 billion feed checks/day from the prompt assumptions.

capacity.label: Latency budget
capacity.value: <= 200 ms end to end, so candidate generation and ranking must be bounded.

capacity.label: Candidate count
capacity.value: Hundreds to low thousands before ranking, tens returned to client.

capacity.label: Training signal
capacity.value: Weighted implicit and explicit reactions, including negative actions.

capacity.label: Core assumptions
capacity.note: About 3 billion total users and 2 billion daily active users. Each active user checks feed roughly twice daily. Feed serving must complete within 200 ms. Training data comes from impressions and reactions.

capacity.label: Design focus
capacity.note: Estimate feed requests and candidate volume. Then explain why the system cannot score every post for every user at request time.

dataModel.name: user_profile_features
dataModel.note: Aggregated user preferences and behavior features.

dataModel.name: candidate_set
dataModel.note: Generated candidates for a feed request.

steps.title: 1. Define the Engagement Objective
steps.description: The model should optimize a weighted engagement score, not raw clicks alone. Positive and negative reactions are labels that teach the system what users value or reject.
steps.decisionPrompt: What target should the ranking model optimize, and why is click-only optimization risky?

concepts.definition: A mathematical target the model learns to optimize.
concepts.whyItMatters: It aligns ranking with product goals.

concepts.definition: Behavior such as clicks or dwell time.
concepts.whyItMatters: It is abundant but noisy.

concepts.definition: Actions such as like, share, hide, or block.
concepts.whyItMatters: It provides stronger but sparser training signal.

deepDives: Clicks can reward clickbait; hides and blocks need negative weight.
bottlenecks: Poor objective design can optimize the wrong behavior.
talkingPoints: Use examples: click=1, like=5, comment=10, share=20, hide=-20, block=-50.

steps.title: 2. Log Training Data and Features
steps.description: Every feed impression and reaction becomes training data. Feature pipelines compute user, post, author-affinity, and context features for both offline training and online serving.
steps.decisionPrompt: How do we collect labels and avoid training-serving skew?

concepts.definition: A record that an item was shown to a user.
concepts.whyItMatters: It provides the denominator for ranking labels.

concepts.definition: A system that stores reusable features for training and serving.
concepts.whyItMatters: It reduces mismatch between offline and online data.

concepts.definition: A difference between features used in training and serving.
concepts.whyItMatters: It can make a good offline model fail online.

deepDives: Log non-clicked impressions too, otherwise the model only sees positives.
bottlenecks: Late or missing logs bias training.
Feature freshness matters for trending posts.

steps.title: 3. Generate a Manageable Candidate Set
steps.description: The system cannot score every post. Candidate generators retrieve a few hundred or thousand plausible posts from social graph, recent activity, topics, and freshness sources.
steps.decisionPrompt: How do we reduce billions of possible posts to a rankable set in milliseconds?

concepts.definition: A recall-oriented stage that finds plausible items quickly.
concepts.whyItMatters: It makes online ranking computationally feasible.

concepts.definition: The chance that relevant items are included in candidates.
concepts.whyItMatters: A ranker cannot recover an item that was never retrieved.

concepts.definition: Combining candidates from multiple retrieval strategies.
concepts.whyItMatters: It balances social, interest, and freshness signals.

deepDives: Candidate generation is optimized for recall and latency; the ranker handles precision.
bottlenecks: A weak generator caps final quality.
Too many candidates break the latency budget.

steps.title: 4. Train a Pointwise Ranking Model
steps.description: A pointwise model predicts reaction probabilities or a weighted engagement score for each user-post pair. The ranking score combines these predictions and business constraints.
steps.decisionPrompt: Should the model compare posts pairwise, listwise, or score each user-post pair independently?

concepts.definition: Training a model to score one item at a time.
concepts.whyItMatters: It is practical for large-scale feed ranking.

concepts.definition: Making predicted probabilities match observed frequencies.
concepts.whyItMatters: It helps combine click, like, comment, and hide predictions.

concepts.definition: A model that predicts several reactions at once.
concepts.whyItMatters: It captures multiple product goals in one ranking stack.

deepDives: The ranker should include negative feedback predictions, not just positive engagement.
bottlenecks: Offline metrics can disagree with online engagement.
talkingPoints: Explain why pointwise is a reasonable interview default.

steps.title: 5. Serve Features Online With Low Latency
steps.description: The ranking service fetches online features for the user and candidates, builds feature vectors, scores them with the model, and returns ordered posts.
steps.decisionPrompt: How do we keep online scoring under 200 ms?

concepts.definition: Low-latency feature storage used during requests.
concepts.whyItMatters: It keeps model inputs fresh and fast.

concepts.definition: Scoring many candidate items in one model invocation.
concepts.whyItMatters: It reduces overhead.

concepts.definition: Default values when a feature is missing or stale.
concepts.whyItMatters: It keeps serving robust.

flows.name: Online ranking
flows.note: The serving path retrieves candidates, fetches features, scores in batch, and returns a page.
diagram: participant S as Online Feature Store

deepDives: Keep model inference and feature fetch bounded; do not run expensive graph traversals in the request path.
bottlenecks: Feature fanout and model server latency.
talkingPoints: State a rough latency budget per stage.

steps.title: 6. Meet the 200 ms Feed Latency Budget
steps.description: Precompute expensive features, cache recent candidate sets, batch model inference, and return enough posts for smooth scrolling without overfetching.
steps.decisionPrompt: Where should computation happen: offline, nearline, or during the request?

concepts.definition: Fresh asynchronous computation from recent streams.
concepts.whyItMatters: It gives fresher signals than batch without blocking requests.

concepts.definition: A per-stage time allocation for the request path.
concepts.whyItMatters: It prevents accidental slow designs.

concepts.definition: Reusing recent candidate or feature results.
concepts.whyItMatters: It lowers serving cost and tail latency.

deepDives: The 200 ms budget is the reason the ranker scores a bounded candidate set, not the entire corpus.
talkingPoints: Use time boxes such as 40 ms candidates, 60 ms features, 50 ms inference, and remaining for hydration/network.

steps.title: 7. Evaluate Offline and Online
steps.description: Offline metrics test ranking quality before launch, while online experiments measure real user impact and guardrail metrics such as hides, blocks, latency, and diversity.
steps.decisionPrompt: How do we know a new feed model is better?

concepts.definition: A ranking metric that rewards relevant items near the top.
concepts.whyItMatters: It matches ordered feed quality better than raw accuracy.

concepts.definition: A controlled online experiment between model versions.
concepts.whyItMatters: It measures actual user behavior.

concepts.definition: A metric that must not regress while optimizing another goal.
concepts.whyItMatters: It prevents engagement wins that harm users or reliability.

deepDives: A model with better offline NDCG can still lose online if it changes user behavior or system latency.
bottlenecks: Feedback loops can bias future training toward what the current model already shows.
talkingPoints: Mention exploration to collect data outside the current model comfort zone.

steps.title: 8. Add Diversity, Negative Feedback, and Ads Controls
steps.description: The final ranking layer applies business and product constraints: hide/block penalties, author or topic diversity, freshness, and sponsored insertion rules.
steps.decisionPrompt: What rules should adjust pure model scores before the feed is returned?

concepts.definition: Rules applied after model scoring.
concepts.whyItMatters: It enforces constraints that are hard or unsafe to leave entirely to the model.

concepts.definition: A rule limiting repeated authors, topics, or media types.
concepts.whyItMatters: It improves feed quality and exploration.

concepts.definition: Controlled placement of ads among organic items.
concepts.whyItMatters: It supports revenue without letting ads dominate the objective.

deepDives: A production feed is not just a model sorted descending; it is a model plus constraints and measurement.
bottlenecks: Too many rules can make model behavior hard to reason about.
Ads constraints can conflict with user engagement.
talkingPoints: Close with the request path and training loop together.

functional.how: Candidate generators retrieve plausible posts, and the ranking model scores user-post pairs with user, post, author, and context features.
Impressions, clicks, likes, comments, shares, hides, and blocks are logged as labels and feature updates for offline training.
Bounded candidates, online feature stores, batch inference, caches, and stage-level latency budgets keep serving within the target.
Post-processing combines model scores with hide/block penalties, diversity constraints, and sponsored insertion rules.

nonFunctional.how: Offline feature stores and model trainers process logged impressions and reactions outside the request path.
Nearline streams update online features for recent user behavior and fast-moving posts.
Offline NDCG, A/B tests, guardrail metrics, and rollout controls prevent unsafe model launches.

finalDesign.title: Final Design
finalDesign.description: The final design is a feed ML platform with an offline training loop and a low-latency serving path. Feed impressions and reactions build labels and features, candidate generators retrieve a bounded set, online feature stores and model servers score candidates, post-processing applies diversity and ads constraints, and experiments close the feedback loop.
finalDesign.decisionPrompt: Walk through one feed request and then explain how that request becomes training data for a future model.

concepts.definition: Candidate generation optimizes recall, while ranking optimizes precision.
concepts.whyItMatters: It is the only practical way to serve personalized feeds at billion-user scale.

concepts.definition: Served items produce impressions and reactions that train future models.
concepts.whyItMatters: It is the engine of continuous feed quality improvement and also a source of bias.

concepts.definition: Metrics and rules that constrain pure engagement optimization.
concepts.whyItMatters: They protect users and product quality from clickbait or repetitive feeds.

options.title: Full Final Design
options.description: Complete architecture combining every major step from the interview. This is the default wrap-up view.

options.title: Precomputed-candidate serving variant
options.description: Precomputing candidates for active users can reduce request latency, but the online ranker still uses fresh features and rules.

flows.name: Online feed request
flows.note: The request path retrieves bounded candidates, scores them, applies rules, hydrates posts, and logs impressions.
diagram: participant S as Online Feature Store

flows.name: Training and rollout loop
flows.note: Interactions become labels and features, then model versions move through offline evaluation and controlled rollout.
diagram: participant O as Offline Feature Store
X->>M: roll out to traffic slice

deepDives: The full design must include the learning loop. A feed ranker without impression logging, feature pipelines, evaluation, and rollout is not a complete ML system.
bottlenecks: Feature fanout and model inference p99.
Feedback-loop bias from only learning on shown items.
Business constraints can conflict with pure engagement ranking.
talkingPoints: Use candidate generation to avoid scoring all posts.
Include negative feedback such as hide and block.

followUps: How do you reduce training-serving skew?
How do you prevent filter bubbles?
How would you handle cold-start users and posts?
Which guardrail metrics would block a rollout?

api.description: Return ranked feed items for a viewer.
diagram: participant F as Online Feature Store

api.description: Log user reactions and negative feedback.

diagram: participant O as Online Feature Store
participant B as Offline Feature Store

diagram: participant A as Model Rollout API

description: Design an ML-powered feed ranking system that serves personalized posts within a 200 ms latency budget.
